content:
# Genesis Chapter 23 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 12v–13r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_023_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_023_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 18–19 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Sarah lived 127 years; these were the years of the life of Sarah.

**2** And Sarah died at Kiriath-arba (that is, Hebron) in the land of Canaan, and Abraham went in to mourn for Sarah and to weep for her.

**3** Then Abraham rose from before his dead and said to the Hittites,

**4** "I am a sojourner and a resident among you. Give me property among you for a burial site, that I may bury my dead out of my sight."

**5** The Hittites answered Abraham,

**6** "Hear us, my lord; you are a prince of God among us. Bury your dead in the choicest of our tombs. None of us will withhold his tomb from you for burying your dead."

**7** Abraham rose and bowed to the people of the land, to the Hittites.

**8** And he spoke with them, saying, "If you are willing that I should bury my dead out of my sight, hear me and entreat for me Ephron the son of Zohar,

**9** that he may give me the cave of Machpelah, which he owns; it is at the end of his field. For the full price let him give it to me in your midst as a possession for a burial site."

**10** Now Ephron was sitting among the Hittites, and Ephron the Hittite answered Abraham in the hearing of the Hittites, of all who went in at the gate of his city,

**11** "No, my lord, hear me: I give you the field, and the cave that is in it I give to you. In the sight of the sons of my people I give it to you. Bury your dead."

**12** Then Abraham bowed down before the people of the land.

**13** And he said to Ephron in the hearing of the people of the land, "But if you will, hear me: I give the price of the field. Accept it from me, that I may bury my dead there."

**14** Ephron answered Abraham,

**15** "My lord, listen to me: a piece of land worth four hundred shekels of silver — what is that between you and me? Bury your dead."

**16** Abraham listened to Ephron, and Abraham weighed out for Ephron the silver that he had named in the hearing of the Hittites: four hundred shekels of silver, according to the weights current among the merchants.

**17** So the field of Ephron in Machpelah, which was to the east of Mamre — the field with the cave that was in it and all the trees that were in the field throughout its whole boundary — was made over

**18** to Abraham as a possession in the sight of the Hittites, before all who went in at the gate of his city.

**19** After this, Abraham buried Sarah his wife in the cave of the field of Machpelah to the east of Mamre (that is, Hebron) in the land of Canaan.

**20** The field and the cave that is in it were made over to Abraham as a possession for a burial site by the Hittites.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 23 spans Folios 12v–13r.

   **Folio 12v** (GEN_023_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 12. This page is presented in inverted orientation in the photograph; the text runs in three columns and carries the closing verses of Genesis 22 in the rightmost column before the chapter transition, with Genesis 23 beginning in that same folio. The petuchah (open paragraph) division marking the transition from Genesis 22 to Genesis 23 is visible in the Masoretic layout. Verses 1 through approximately 13 of Genesis 23 are legible on this page. The Masoretic pointing (niqqud) and cantillation marks (te'amim) are clearly visible throughout.

   **Folio 13r** (GEN_023_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 13. This folio carries Genesis 23:14–20 through to the opening of Genesis 24. The legal transfer language of verses 17–20 is clearly set out in the three-column Tiberian format. The folio number "13" is visible in the upper margin of the page photograph.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Page 18 (GEN_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) carries the chapter heading "CAP. XXV" at the top margin, marking the beginning of the LXX chapter that corresponds to Genesis 23. The continuous uncial text of the Vaticanus runs in three columns; the chapter carries through Genesis 23 and into the transition to Genesis 24. Page 19 (GEN_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries the continuation of the Vaticanus text, with the chapter marker "CAP. XXVI" visible in the left margin, marking the LXX division for the next section (corresponding to Genesis 24). The LXX text was read against the MT at every significant translation decision for Genesis 23.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. וַיִּהְיוּ חַיֵּי שָׂרָה — "The Years of the Life of Sarah" (v. 1) — The Unusual Repetition**

The verse opens with one of the most structurally distinctive formulas in the patriarchal narratives:

וַיִּהְיוּ חַיֵּי שָׂרָה מֵאָה שָׁנָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים
(vayyihyu hayyei Sarah me'ah shanah ve'esrim shanah vesheva shanim)
"And the life of Sarah was one hundred years and twenty years and seven years"

שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי שָׂרָה
(shnei hayyei Sarah)
"the years of the life of Sarah"

The Hebrew gives the age in three components — 100 + 20 + 7 — rather than simply "127 years." Rabbinic tradition (Genesis Rabbah 58:1) interprets this breakdown as meaningful: at 100 Sarah was as beautiful as at 20 in terms of sin (blameless, as a 20-year-old is not accountable to the heavenly court), and at 20 she was as beautiful as at 7. Whether or not one follows the midrashic reading, the disaggregated form does give special weight to each segment of her years. The formal translation renders the total "127 years" (following ESV, NASB, NIV) as the most natural English form, while preserving the structural phrase "these were the years of the life of Sarah" — the repeated formula שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי שָׂרָה at the close of the verse — which echoes the patriarchal death notices (cf. Genesis 25:7, "These are the years of the life of Abraham"). Sarah is the only woman in the Hebrew Bible to receive this formula.

The Masoretic tradition marks the word וְלִבְכֹּתָהּ ("and to weep for her," v. 2) with a small כ (kaf ketana, "small kaf") — one of the scribal notations in the Leningrad Codex. The traditional interpretation is that the mourning for Sarah, though genuine, was less than the mourning Abraham would later offer, because Sarah had died in Hebron while Abraham was in Beer-sheba; or alternatively, the small letter signals the brevity of the weeping, or that grief is subordinated to action (Abraham acts, he does not only mourn). The formal translation notes this scribal marking but renders the text without adjustment.

**2. וַיָּקָם אַבְרָהָם מֵעַל פְּנֵי מֵתוֹ — "Abraham Rose from Before His Dead" (v. 3)**

The idiom וַיָּקָם מֵעַל פְּנֵי מֵתוֹ (vayyaqam me'al pnei meto, "and he rose from before/upon the face of his dead one") is formally unusual and evocative. פְּנֵי (pnei, "face/before") with מֵת (met, "dead one") suggests Abraham was prostrate before or over Sarah's body — the classic posture of mourning in the ancient Near East. The phrase "his dead" (מֵתוֹ, meto) — using the possessive with the noun for a dead person — will recur six times in this chapter as a refrain, always on Abraham's lips. The formal translation renders "his dead" throughout as a deliberate lexical choice, preserving the intimate possessive.

**3. גֵּר וְתוֹשָׁב אָנֹכִי עִמָּכֶם — "I Am a Sojourner and a Resident Among You" (v. 4)**

This is the key legal declaration that frames the entire chapter. Abraham defines his status with two terms:

גֵּר (ger) — a sojourner, alien, stranger; one who dwells among a people to whom he does not belong by birth or covenant
תוֹשָׁב (toshav) — a resident, settler; one who has taken up long-term dwelling in a place

The pairing גֵּר וְתוֹשָׁב (ger vetoshav) occurs here and in Leviticus 25:35, 47, where it designates a legally recognized status of resident alien — one with certain protections and obligations under the social order without being a full citizen. The phrase captures a fundamental tension: Abraham is both an outsider (ger) and a long-term dweller (toshav) in Canaan. He has lived there for decades; he does not own land there. To negotiate the purchase of a burial site is thus both a practical necessity and a juridical act — the first acquisition of territory in the land of promise.

The formal translation renders "sojourner and resident" as the most precise English equivalent, following NASB ("stranger and sojourner") as the traditional rendering but preferring "sojourner and resident" to reflect the distinct legal nuances of each term. ESV renders "sojourner and foreigner."

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders: πάροικος καὶ παρεπίδημος (paroikos kai parepidemos — "sojourner and pilgrim/stranger"), using two Greek terms that together convey the same legal-social duality. These Greek terms reappear in the NT: the author of 1 Peter 2:11 addresses his readers as παροίκους καὶ παρεπιδήμους (paroikous kai parepidemos — "sojourners and exiles/pilgrims"), almost certainly echoing Abraham's self-identification here; and Hebrews 11:13 says the patriarchs "acknowledged that they were strangers and exiles on the earth" (ξένοι καὶ παρεπίδημοί, xenoi kai parepidemioi). The formal translation's choice of "sojourner and resident" preserves both terms as distinct.

**4. נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים אַתָּה בְּתוֹכֵנוּ — "You Are a Prince of God Among Us" (v. 6)**

The Hittites address Abraham as נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים (nesi' Elohim, "a prince/chieftain of God"). This honorific is significant and much discussed:

- נָשִׂיא (nasi') is a title for a tribal leader, chief, or prince — it is the word used for the tribal leaders in Numbers (the "princes" of the twelve tribes).
- The construct phrase נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים can be rendered either as "a prince of God" (i.e., one exalted by God, divinely appointed) or as a superlative "a mighty prince" (Elohim occasionally functioning as a superlative intensifier in Hebrew, as in "a mighty wind" = רוּחַ אֱלֹהִים in Genesis 1:2 by some interpretations; cf. also "cedars of God" = cedars of exceptional greatness in Psalm 80:10).

The major translations divide: KJV renders "a mighty prince" (taking Elohim as superlative); ESV/NASB render "a prince of God" (taking Elohim as a genitive of divine association); NIV renders "a mighty prince." The LXX (Vaticanus) renders βασιλεὺς παρὰ θεοῦ (basileus para theou — "a king from/before God"), taking the phrase as a divine origin construction rather than a superlative.

The formal translation renders "a prince of God" — following ESV and the genitive reading — because the narrative context suggests the Hittites recognize something divine about Abraham, not merely that he is great among men. Their deference throughout the chapter goes beyond ordinary courtesy to a man of great wealth; the specific invocation of Elohim gives the honorific its particular texture. A "prince of God" is more precise to the Hebrew than merely "a mighty prince."

**5. The Negotiation Structure (vv. 3–16) — Ancient Near Eastern Real Estate Protocol**

The chapter is a masterclass in ancient Near Eastern negotiation rhetoric, and the formal translation preserves every move in the exchange:

- **Move 1 (vv. 3–4):** Abraham rises, defines his legal status (ger vetoshav), and makes a general request for "property for a burial site" without specifying which property or from whom.
- **Move 2 (vv. 5–6):** The Hittites respond with effusive honor and a blanket offer: "choose any of our tombs." This public, communal offer sounds generous but obligates no one specifically. It is the conventional opening of polite negotiation — the offer that sounds total but commits nothing.
- **Move 3 (vv. 7–9):** Abraham bows in thanks, then names his actual desire: the cave of Machpelah, the property of Ephron son of Zohar. He asks the community to intercede with Ephron. He proposes payment at "full price" — signaling he will pay, not expect a gift — which is itself a negotiating position.
- **Move 4 (vv. 10–11):** Ephron, who was already present in the assembly ("sitting among the Hittites"), responds not to the community but directly to Abraham, in the hearing of all. He offers not just the cave but the entire field — and offers it as a gift: "I give it to you." This is the classic counter-move of honorable negotiation: an extravagant offer that appears generous but shifts the dynamic to one of gift and obligation.
- **Move 5 (vv. 12–13):** Abraham bows again, then declines the gift: "I give the price of the field." He insists on purchase, pressing the point of payment.
- **Move 6 (vv. 14–15):** Ephron names his price — 400 shekels of silver — but embeds it in a dismissive phrase: "what is that between you and me?" The question is rhetorically polished: the sum is named (it is a very large sum), but stated as if it were trivial between men of their standing. This is a named price with plausible deniability.
- **Move 7 (v. 16):** Abraham "listened to Ephron" — the verb שָׁמַע (shama') is the same word used for obedience and agreement throughout the OT — and "weighed out" the silver at the named price. The transaction is complete.

The formal translation preserves each speech as a distinct move, rendering every politeness formula ("hear me," "my lord," "in your midst," "in the sight of the sons of my people") without flattening the negotiation to a simple bargain. These formulas are load-bearing: they are the language of honorable public transaction in a society where shame and honor govern economic life.

**6. מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה — "The Cave of Machpelah" (vv. 9, 17, 19) — The Name**

The name מַכְפֵּלָה (Machpelah) is of uncertain etymology. The most commonly proposed derivation is from the root כָּפַל (kafal, "to double, to fold"), suggesting "the double cave" or "the cave of the double [portion]." Others have proposed it is a place name with no transparent etymology. The site (מְעָרַת הַמַּכְפֵּלָה, me'arat haMakhpelah, "the cave of Machpelah") will become the patriarchal family burial ground: Abraham himself (25:9–10), Sarah (here), Isaac and Rebekah (35:27–29; 49:31), Jacob and Leah (49:29–33; 50:13). The formal translation renders "the cave of Machpelah" as a proper name throughout, as the text presents it, without interpretation.

The Vaticanus LXX renders: τὸ σπήλαιον τὸ διπλοῦν (to spelaion to diploun — "the double cave"), reading the name as a descriptive: "the double cave." This LXX reading supports the kafal ("to double") etymology. The formal translation follows the MT and preserves "Machpelah" as a proper name.

**7. עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר — "Current Among the Merchants" (v. 16)**

The phrase בְּכֶסֶף עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר (bekheseph over lassokher, "silver passing/current to the merchant") describes a commercial weight standard:

עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר (over lassokher) — literally "passing among/to the merchant" — refers to silver that circulates as an accepted, standardized weight in commercial transactions. Before coinage (coinage comes to the ancient Near East only in the Persian period, well after this narrative setting), transactions in silver were conducted by weight, using standardized units. The phrase effectively means "accepted-weight silver" or "commercial-grade silver" — silver whose weight has been verified against the standard merchant's scale.

The 400-shekel sum is large by any measure. Archaeological and textual comparisons suggest that in the second millennium BCE, a laborer's annual wage was approximately 8–10 shekels of silver; 400 shekels would thus represent roughly 40–50 years of wages. Several scholars note that the purchase price appears deliberately high — either reflecting an actual price for a prime property, or possibly a literary number (400 = a round large number, cf. the 400 men with Esau in Genesis 33:1, the 400-year prediction of Genesis 15:13). The formal translation renders "four hundred shekels of silver, according to the weights current among the merchants" — preserving the technical commercial phrase.

The LXX (Vaticanus) renders: τετρακοσίους διδράχμους ἀργυρίου (tetrakosious didrachmus arguriou — "four hundred drachmas of silver"), translating the shekel with the Greek coin/weight equivalent didrachmon (a two-drachma weight). The LXX substitution of a Greek commercial standard for the Hebrew merchant-weight formula is a naturalizing translation move, replacing an unfamiliar ancient commercial term with a recognizable Greek one.

**8. The Legal Transfer Formula (vv. 17–18, 20) — The Deed of Purchase**

Verses 17–20 constitute the legal record of the property transfer, and their language is the most formally precise in the chapter:

וַיָּקָם שְׂדֵה עֶפְרוֹן אֲשֶׁר בַּמַּכְפֵּלָה...
לְאַבְרָהָם לְמִקְנָה...
לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי חֵת בְּכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר עִירוֹ

(vayyaqam sedeh Ephron asher baMakhpelah... le'Avraham lemiqnah... le'einei venei Khet bekhol ba'ei sha'ar iro)
"The field of Ephron... was made over to Abraham as a possession... in the sight of the sons of Heth, before all who entered the gate of his city"

The verb קוּם in the Qal (qam, "to rise, to stand firm") here functions as a legal term: "was confirmed / was legally established." The phrase לְמִקְנָה (lemiqnah, "as a purchase / as a possession") derives from קָנָה (qanah, "to acquire, to purchase") — the same root as the Canaanite divine name El Qoneh (God the Creator/Acquirer; Genesis 14:22). The property is עָבַר לְאַבְרָהָם (avar le'Avraham, "passed to Abraham") through a transaction witnessed בְּכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר עִירוֹ (bekhol ba'ei sha'ar iro, "before all who entered the gate of his city") — the gate of the city was the legal public forum of the ancient Near East, where transactions, judgments, and official business were conducted (cf. Ruth 4:1–12, the Boaz-kinsman-redeemer transaction at the gate).

The formal translation carefully renders this legal formula in verses 17–18 and echoes it in the concluding summary of verse 20, preserving the repetition as the MT has it. The repetition is not redundancy — it is the documentary double-confirmation characteristic of ancient legal drafting.

**9. אֲשֶׁר לִפְנֵי מַמְרֵא — "To the East of Mamre" (vv. 17, 19)**

The formula אֲשֶׁר עַל פְּנֵי מַמְרֵא (asher al pnei Mamre, literally "which is on the face/before Mamre") identifies the geographical location of the field. עַל פְּנֵי (al pnei, "before, to the face of, opposite") is a directional/positional formula in Hebrew that can mean "to the east of" or "facing" a landmark. The formal translation renders "to the east of Mamre," following the standard interpretive tradition (ESV, NASB, KJV) that locates Machpelah east of Mamre (ancient Mamre/Hebron being the patriarchal base of operations in Canaan). The parenthetical identification "that is, Hebron" in verse 19 is the MT's own gloss — the narrator identifies the ancient site for the reader.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 23

The Vaticanus manuscript pages 18–19 (GEN_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg and GEN_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) were read directly. The chapter heading "CAP. XXV" at the top of page 18 reflects the LXX division, which numbers this as the 25th section. The LXX of Genesis 23 follows the MT closely but carries several noteworthy renderings:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 23:1 | מֵאָה שָׁנָה וְעֶשְׂרִים שָׁנָה וְשֶׁבַע שָׁנִים (100 + 20 + 7 years) | ἑκατὸν εἴκοσι ἑπτὰ ἔτη (127 years — combined total) | "127 years" | LXX combines the MT's three-part enumeration into a single number; formal translation follows the MT structure in acknowledging the parts but rendering the total |
| 23:4 | גֵּר וְתוֹשָׁב (ger vetoshav — "sojourner and resident") | πάροικος καὶ παρεπίδημος (paroikos kai parepidemos — "sojourner and pilgrim") | "sojourner and resident" | LXX uses terms echoed in 1 Pet 2:11 and Heb 11:13 for believers as pilgrims on earth |
| 23:6 | נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים (nesi' Elohim — "prince of God" / "mighty prince") | βασιλεὺς παρὰ θεοῦ (basileus para theou — "a king from God") | "prince of God" | LXX elevates Abraham's title to "king" and reads the construction as divine origin ("from God") rather than superlative; formal translation follows MT's "prince" |
| 23:9 | בְּכֶסֶף מָלֵא (bekhesef male' — "for full silver / full price") | ἀργυρίου ἀξίου (arguriou axiou — "for worthy/appropriate silver") | "for the full price" | LXX renders the Hebrew idiom interpretively; formal translation follows MT |
| 23:15 | אַרְבַּע מֵאוֹת שֶׁקֶל כֶּסֶף (400 shekels of silver) | τετρακοσίους διδράχμους ἀργυρίου (400 drachmas of silver) | "400 shekels of silver" | LXX naturalizes the weight standard with the Greek didrachmon; formal translation preserves the Hebrew "shekels" |
| 23:16 | עֹבֵר לַסֹּחֵר (over lassokher — "current among the merchants") | δοκίμου τοῖς ἐμπόροις (dokimou tois emporois — "approved/tested among the merchants") | "current among the merchants" | LXX uses δόκιμος (dokimos, "tested, approved, proven") — a more precise commercial term; the sense is equivalent: silver whose weight has been verified. Formal translation follows MT's idiom |
| 23:17 | וַיָּקָם (vayyaqam — "and it was confirmed/established") | ἐκυρώθη (ekurōthē — "it was ratified/confirmed") | "was made over" | LXX uses κυρόω (kuroō, "to confirm, ratify, validate") — an explicit legal term that makes the juridical force unmistakable; formal translation renders "was made over" following ESV |

The most significant LXX variant in this chapter is the rendering of נְשִׂיא אֱלֹהִים (nesi' Elohim) as βασιλεὺς παρὰ θεοῦ ("a king from God") in verse 6. The LXX elevates Abraham's title from "prince/chief" to "king" and reads the genitive as origin rather than superlative, producing a quite different portrait of Abraham's standing among the Hittites. The formal translation follows the MT's "prince of God" as the more conservative reading.

The LXX also renders the legal verb וַיָּקָם with the explicitly juridical κυρόω — the same verb used in Galatians 3:15, 17 for the "ratification" of a covenant. The legal precision of the LXX's rendering of the property transfer language reflects the Greek legal context in which the Septuagint was produced.

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**Sarah as the First Named Burial in the Patriarchal Narrative**

Genesis 23 is the only chapter of the patriarchal narrative devoted entirely to a burial transaction. Sarah is the first person in the Bible whose death, mourning, and burial receive extended narrative treatment. She is also the only woman in the Hebrew Bible whose age at death is recorded using the patriarchal formula (שְׁנֵי חַיֵּי, "the years of the life of"). Her burial inaugurates the cave of Machpelah as the patriarchal family tomb — a site that will receive five more burials over the coming chapters.

**The Cave of Machpelah as the First Foothold in the Promised Land**

The entire chapter turns on a juridical irony. God has promised Abraham the land of Canaan as an inheritance (Genesis 12:7; 13:15; 15:7, 18–21; 17:8); yet Abraham has lived in Canaan for decades as a landless resident (ger vetoshav). In the entire narrative, Genesis 23 records the first time Abraham actually owns a piece of the promised land — and he purchases it not as a home but as a grave. The first Israelite title deed in Canaan is a burial plot. The narrative weight of this is considerable: the patriarchs die as sojourners, but they are buried in the land of promise, an act of faith that the promise will be fulfilled (cf. Hebrews 11:13–16 on the patriarchs who "greeted the promises from a far").

**The Repetition of "His Dead" — A Refrain of Grief**

The phrase מֵתוֹ (meto, "his dead") appears six times in the chapter (vv. 3, 4, 6, 8, 11, 13, 15) as Abraham's consistent designation for Sarah. The Hebrew legal term for the transaction never loses sight of its human context: every reference to the commercial process is anchored to the specific person for whose burial Abraham is negotiating. The formal translation preserves "his dead" throughout as a deliberate refrain, acknowledging both the legal and the personal register simultaneously.

**The Witness Formula — "In the Sight of All Who Entered the Gate"**

The legal transfer in verses 17–18 and its summary in verse 20 both invoke the witness formula לְעֵינֵי בְנֵי חֵת בְּכֹל בָּאֵי שַׁעַר עִירוֹ ("in the sight of the sons of Heth, before all who entered the gate of his city"). The city gate was the public legal forum — the place where transactions were announced, recorded, and witnessed by the community. To conduct a purchase "in the gate" with named witnesses was the ancient equivalent of a recorded deed. The narrative presents the Machpelah purchase as a fully legal, fully public, fully witnessed acquisition — one that could not later be disputed. The formal translation preserves both instances of this formula, since the repetition itself carries legal significance: the transaction is confirmed twice, first in the narration of the event and then in the concluding summary.

---

— LOGOS
